channel.title: Breast Cancer Recovery on MinWeb
channel.description: Recent content in Breast Cancer Recovery on MinWeb

item.title: Post-Mastectomy Pain Syndrome: Why You Aren't Alone
item.description:
You survived one of the most frightening diagnoses a person can face. You underwent a mastectomy to remove cancer, did exactly what the doctors ordered, and expected a quick recovery. But instead of healing, you are left with a terrifying new reality: constant, stabbing, electric-shock pain. Simple things—wearing a shirt, sitting in a chair, or hugging a loved one—now feel like torture.

If this sounds familiar, or if you are staring at a medical chart feeling completely unheard, you aren’t alone. And more importantly, you aren’t crazy.

item.title: Post-Mastectomy Pain Syndrome: Why Chronic Pain Persists
item.description:
For many, a mastectomy is strictly about survival. We focus on removing the cancer to guarantee a future, and we celebrate those life-saving results. Rightly so. But there is a silent chapter that begins after the cancer is gone and the hospital discharge papers are signed. For too many women, the hardest part of the journey isn’t the diagnosis—it’s the debilitating, chronic pain that follows.

If you’ve been told that persistent pain after surgery is just “part of the healing process,” know this: you aren’t losing your mind. An investigative report published on April 4, 2026, by USA TODAY and KFF Health News finally put a name to this condition that’s been hiding in plain sight: Post-Mastectomy Pain Syndrome (PMPS). Honestly, it’s about time someone started talking about this openly.

item.title: The Hidden Chronic Pain After Mastectomy Surgery
item.description:
For thousands of women, a mastectomy is a life-saving decision. It’s a courageous act to stop cancer and reclaim a future. But for a staggering number of these patients, the surgery isn’t the end of the battle; it’s the beginning of a silent, agonizing struggle that most surgeons never prepare them for.

Imagine waking up from a life-altering surgery only to find your own body feels like the enemy. You expect some discomfort, sure, but then the “stabbing” begins. Weeks pass, and simple, everyday actions—like lifting a fork, putting on a shirt, or even feeling the soft brush of a pet—send jolts of electricity radiating through your chest and back. This isn’t just “post-surgical soreness.” It is Post-Mastectomy Pain Syndrome (PMPS). Honestly, it’s infuriating that so many women are left to figure this out on their own. For far too many, this becomes a permanent, devastating reality.
